description: Baseline acceptance score with honest mixed dynamic/static bridge evaluation in autoresearch.sh.

asi.hypothesis: Establish an acceptance benchmark first so progress can be measured without crashing on incomplete bridge features.
baseline_observations: cargo test and CLI help already pass
only one webhook route is wired in src/bot_server.rs
Discord/Feishu/QQ handlers exist but are not exposed coherently
asi.next_action_hint: Add executable review coverage and wire multi-platform webhook routing through a shared bridge path.

description: Wire all four webhook routes through a shared auto-session bridge and add executable reviewer coverage.

asi.hypothesis: A usable bridge needs one shared incoming-message path plus executable reviewer tests, not four ad-hoc webhook flows.
wins: bot_server now exposes telegram/discord/feishu/qq routes
manager now auto-creates sessions and respects per-session reply_target
review_bridge integration tests exercise routing and session reuse
main can initialize Discord, Feishu, and QQ channel adapters coherently
remaining_gaps: benchmark dynamic score is flat because it still only checks cargo test and help
docs still likely drift from the simplified startup model
no config-file driven startup or persistent sessions in the current binary path
asi.next_action_hint: Improve user-facing usability with truthful docs/startup flow and consider adding dynamic readiness checks beyond static route counting.

description: Replace the broken startup path with a dry-run-able launcher and rewrite the user docs/review guidance to match the real runtime.

asi.hypothesis: A usable bridge needs a truthful startup path and docs that match the actual binary, plus a dry-run check that can be evaluated automatically.
wins: start.sh now validates and launches the real binary via environment variables
benchmark now checks startup_ok dynamically via AGENTIM_DRY_RUN=1 ./start.sh
README/QUICK_START/SETUP/BOT_INTEGRATION now describe the actual single-command runtime and review/eval loop
removed help-time warnings from unnecessary bots re-exports
asi.benchmark_note: This run intentionally hardened the benchmark by adding startup_ok and by counting the newly exposed credential flags in --help; the score increase is tied to a real launcher and truthful docs, not docs-only padding.
remaining_gaps: binary still routes everything to one default agent instead of configurable multi-agent policies
session persistence exists only as scaffolding and is not wired into runtime
webhook signature verification is still missing
asi.next_action_hint: Add a real multi-agent routing configuration or persistence-backed runtime behavior that improves bridge usefulness beyond startup/docs polish.

description: Add per-platform agent overrides so different IM routes can be bridged to different registered agents, with reviewer coverage.

asi.hypothesis: To be a real bridge for various agents, the binary needs platform-specific agent selection instead of one global default agent.
wins: bot_server now carries a BotServerConfig with per-platform agent ids
CLI and start.sh support telegram/discord/feishu/qq agent overrides
bot handlers accept the selected agent id instead of hardcoding default-agent
review_bridge now proves different routes can return different agent-specific responses
asi.benchmark_note: Dynamic score was tightened again by counting the new agent-routing help flags and by requiring the configured-agent review test to pass.
remaining_gaps: routing is per-platform, not yet per-user/workspace/policy
persistence scaffolding still is not wired into runtime
webhook authenticity checks are still absent
asi.next_action_hint: Use the next iteration on persistence-backed sessions or lightweight routing policies that increase real-world usefulness without overcomplicating the binary.

description: Add optional session persistence to the runtime and reviewer coverage for restart recovery.

asi.hypothesis: A usable IM bridge should survive restarts; wiring a lightweight state file into the runtime is a high-value next step.
wins: AgentIM can now save and reload serialized sessions from a JSON state file
bot_server persists sessions after successful webhook handling when state_file is configured
CLI/start.sh/docs expose --state-file and AGENTIM_STATE_FILE
review_bridge now verifies restart recovery through a persisted session file
asi.benchmark_note: Dynamic score increased by counting the new --state-file help flag and by requiring the persistence reviewer test to pass.
remaining_gaps: persistence is full-session JSON snapshots, not incremental or crash-safe storage
agent routing is still per-platform rather than policy-based
webhook signature verification and auth hardening are still missing
asi.next_action_hint: Tackle webhook authenticity checks or a lightweight policy router next, but keep the binary understandable.

description: Add an optional shared webhook secret guard and reviewer coverage for protected routes.

asi.hypothesis: A usable bridge should have at least one simple, deterministic way to protect webhook routes before implementing full platform signature verification.
wins: bot_server can now require x-agentim-secret when webhook_secret is configured
CLI/start.sh/docs expose the shared secret path consistently
review_bridge now proves missing or wrong secrets are rejected with 401
kept the hardening lightweight and uniform across all routes
asi.benchmark_note: Dynamic score increased by counting the new --webhook-secret help flag and by requiring the security reviewer test to pass.
remaining_gaps: shared-secret auth is only a lightweight guard, not full platform-native signature verification
routing is still per-platform instead of user/workspace policy based
persistence is snapshot-based rather than transactional
asi.next_action_hint: Either deepen security with platform-specific signature adapters or improve routing ergonomics with policy/config support, whichever yields more user value per complexity.

description: Add machine-readable health/review endpoints so external automation can inspect bridge status safely.

asi.hypothesis: If the bridge must be continuously reviewed/evaluated, it should expose machine-readable runtime status instead of relying only on logs and docs.
wins: bot_server now exposes /healthz and /reviewz
reviewz reports session count, registered resources, per-platform agent mapping, and whether persistence/security are enabled
ops endpoints respect the same shared secret guard when configured
review_bridge now checks the status endpoints end-to-end
asi.benchmark_note: Dynamic score increased by requiring the ops reviewer test to pass; the score gain comes from an executable status surface for automation.
remaining_gaps: route selection is still coarse-grained per platform, not policy-driven
webhook security is shared-secret based rather than platform-native signatures
persistence is still snapshot-oriented
asi.next_action_hint: Focus next on either a small policy router or safer persistence semantics, whichever can be added without making startup UX confusing.

description: Add a binary-level dry-run mode so startup can be validated directly from the CLI, with reviewer coverage.

asi.hypothesis: Usability improves if startup validation works directly from the binary, not only through the shell wrapper.
wins: CLI now exposes --dry-run for direct startup validation
main validates configuration and exits before binding the server in dry-run mode
review_bridge runs the compiled binary and verifies dry-run exits cleanly
README/QUICK_START now show both shell and binary dry-run paths
asi.benchmark_note: Dynamic score increased by counting the new --dry-run flag and by requiring the binary dry-run reviewer test to pass.
remaining_gaps: config is still split across flags/env vars instead of a dedicated runtime config file
security uses a shared secret rather than platform-native signature verification
persistence writes whole snapshots
asi.next_action_hint: A runtime config file is now the clearest next usability win if more iteration time is available.

description: Add runtime JSON config loading so the bridge can start from a real config file, not only flags/env vars.

asi.hypothesis: The clearest remaining usability win is a real runtime config file that matches the current binary, so setup is not spread only across flags and environment variables.
wins: binary now supports --config-file and start.sh supports AGENTIM_CONFIG_FILE
runtime config merges with CLI flags, which still take precedence
agentim.json.example now matches the actual runtime schema instead of an obsolete CLI model
review_bridge proves config-driven dry-run startup works end-to-end
asi.benchmark_note: Dynamic score increased by counting the new --config-file flag and by requiring the runtime-config reviewer test to pass.
remaining_gaps: config is still flat JSON rather than a richer policy/routing DSL
security remains shared-secret based instead of platform-native signature verification
persistence still saves full snapshots
asi.next_action_hint: If continuing, focus on one of three deeper improvements: policy-based routing, atomic/incremental persistence, or platform-native signature verification.

description: Harden session snapshot persistence with temp-file writes and reviewer coverage for clean saves.

asi.hypothesis: Persistence is more trustworthy if snapshots are written via temp files and promoted atomically enough for the local filesystem, instead of overwriting the target file directly.
wins: save_sessions_to_path now writes to a sibling temp file before rename
review_bridge verifies saved snapshots remain valid JSON and do not leave temp artifacts behind
improves the runtime path without changing the external UX
asi.benchmark_note: Dynamic score increased by requiring the clean-snapshot persistence reviewer test to pass.
remaining_gaps: snapshot persistence is still whole-file rather than incremental
security is still shared-secret based rather than platform-native signatures
routing remains per-platform rather than policy-based
asi.next_action_hint: The next major frontier is policy-based routing or platform-native request verification; both are larger jumps than the recent polish work.
